metric_name: Linux development speed vs. commercial OS
metric_value: Linux developing faster than any other commercial OS to date
notes: Claybrook's core assessment; development velocity was observable metric

metric_value: Linux will leapfrog Windows to replace Unix on the high end within 7-8 years (by 2008-2009)
notes: Bold prediction for 2001; Unix was still dominant in enterprise high-end

metric_value: IDC 2004 predicted Windows would overtake Unix by 2008; Linux grew from 8.4% to 14.9% server share by 2008; Unix declined from 39.6% to 31.9%
notes: Unix displacement broadly confirmed; Linux took Unix's place as growth platform

metric_name: Linux as primary server OS by decade end
metric_value: Linux will join IBM z/OS and Microsoft Windows as the primary server OSes before end of decade (by 2010)

metric_name: Linux primary server OS status - actual outcome
metric_value: Linux server revenue reached $1.6B quarterly by 2005 with 14th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth; Linux became dominant public cloud OS by 2010
notes: Prediction confirmed; Linux became dominant server/cloud OS by 2010

metric_value: Compaq, HP, IBM and many smaller companies contributing to Linux development efforts
IBM positioned both z/OS (mainframe) and Linux as strategic platforms — Linux for distributed; z/OS for mainframe
notes: Aberdeen correctly identified IBM's dual-platform strategy

metric_name: Unix high-end market position at study time
metric_value: Unix occupying high-end server market that Linux is predicted to displace
notes: Unix had 39.6% server market share at time; Linux had only 8.4%

metric_value: Linux server market share reached 28.3% of server sales by 2004 per IDC (combined Unix+Linux 1.48M units)
notes: IDC 2004 data confirms rapid Linux growth from negligible 2001 share

metric_value: Linux server revenue grew 63% to $960 million in Q4 2003 (IDC)
